name: Priya (engaged grad student)
notes: Engaged HCI 271 student, has concrete examples for every section, names teammates, gives 1-5 ratings with justifications. Designed to drive the form-mode bot through every section so the resulting transcript is the GOLDEN ARTIFACT for acceptance F2/F4/F5. If the bot probes once on a thin answer, the next turn deepens it.
turns: Hi! I'm Priya from Team Coyote. Yeah, ready to walk through Week 6.

The thing that stuck with me was the line between an observation and an insight. Specifically a moment in lecture where Magy contrasted 'the user clicked the back button' (observation) with 'the user lost trust in the page' (insight). I had been writing all our field notes as observations and calling them findings, which now feels lazy.

Concrete moment — in our Tuesday synthesis session, Marco pointed at a sticky that said 'participant returned to homepage three times' and asked 'so what?' That 'so what' became our team's joke for the rest of the week, and it forced every observation to earn its way to becoming an insight. We threw out about 40% of our stickies once we applied that filter.
Nothing more on that one — let's move on.

We did affinity diagramming on Wednesday for about three hours, with maybe 180 stickies from our user interviews. Theory said cluster by behavior; practice was way messier — we kept defaulting to clustering by topic ('signup flow', 'navigation', 'errors') because that's how our notes were already organized.

Definitely hit the 'clustering by topic instead of behavior' pitfall, and 'one person driving the wall' for the first 45 minutes — Devi was moving stickies faster than the rest of us could read them, and we had to literally tell her to step back. Once she did, the clusters got more honest. We also went abstract too fast on one cluster called 'frustration' — too generic, eventually broke it into three behavior-rooted ones.
Move on, yeah.
I thought affinity diagramming was basically grouping similar quotes — the kind of thing you could do in a spreadsheet in an hour. I treated it like coding qualitative data with a fancier name.

I was surprised by how much the *physical* act of moving the sticky mattered — a sticky in your hand on the wall is something you have to defend by where you put it. In a spreadsheet you just type a category and move on. The friction of moving the sticky forced us to actually argue.
I'm still uncertain when to stop clustering. We made it through three rounds of regrouping and I genuinely could not tell whether round three was insight or just fatigue.
Move on.

It changes how I'm going to write our next round of interview questions. Up until now I was writing questions that surface attitudes — what users think — and treating those as primary. After this week I think we need at least half our questions to be behavioral probes that get them to walk through what they actually did, because the gap between say-vs-do is where the real insights are.

Smallest concrete change: in next week's interviews I'll add a 'walk me through the last time you actually did X' prompt to every question that currently asks 'what do you think about X'. Concrete and easy to check on Friday.
Move on.

Honestly we did NOT have a shared understanding at the start. Monday standup was 25 minutes and we left thinking we were aligned, but by Tuesday afternoon Marco and I were doing duplicative coding while Devi and Yusuf were already affinity-clustering. We caught it Wednesday morning and had to redo about an hour of Marco's work.
We adapted by adding a mid-week 15-minute sync on Wednesday morning and writing tasks on a shared board with a single-owner rule. Right call — it cost 15 minutes and saved hours.
Nothing more on planning.
Roster: Priya (me), Marco, Devi, Yusuf — four of us total.
I drove the user interview synthesis — coding 60 of the 180 stickies and writing the first-pass cluster labels.
Marco did the bulk of the raw note transcription from the interviews and was the team's 'so what' challenger during clustering. Probably the most underrated contribution because it kept us honest.
Devi led the affinity wall physically and started the first three clusters. Strong start, but as I mentioned earlier she was moving too fast for the first 45 min — once we rebalanced it was great.

Yusuf focused on the literature side — he read the Braun and Clarke paper and the NN/g pitfalls article and brought specific terminology to our debrief. He synthesized less data but raised the methodological bar.

Distribution was mostly equitable but Yusuf did less hands-on synthesis than the rest of us. If I could change one thing, I'd have asked him to drive one of the four clusters end to end so he gets the same hands-on reps.
Move on.

What worked: the Wednesday 15-minute sync I mentioned. Specifically, we ended it by each saying one sentence about what we were going to do before lunch, and that one-sentence rule killed the ambiguity that was eating Tuesday.

What broke down: Tuesday afternoon Slack thread on whether to merge two clusters. We had four people typing past each other for 40 minutes, no decision, and I think two of us walked away thinking we'd merged and two thinking we hadn't. Impact was that on Wednesday we were working from two different versions of the wall and had to reconcile.
Improvement next week: any decision that takes more than five Slack messages becomes a 5-minute synchronous call. Measurable — I can count Slack threads on Friday and check.
Move on.
Ready for the ratings.
Justification first: by midweek we had a clear shared goal but Monday we did not. Net I'd give it a 3.
Justification: everyone got airtime, but Yusuf's methodological contributions felt slightly undervalued in the moment because they didn't translate to sticky counts. 4.
Justification: the Slack thread incident proves we still resolve disagreements by attrition rather than constructively. The rest of the week was fine. 3.
Justification: we hit our internal Friday deadline for the affinity wall photo. 5.
Justification: I am confident about our synthesis but uncertain about whether our clusters point to a coherent design direction. 4.
Move on.

Our biggest open question is whether the three insight clusters we landed on — trust, recovery from errors, and self-paced exploration — actually map to one coherent user need or whether we're seeing three different user types and need to segment.
Why that one: because it's the bottleneck for our design concept. If they're one need we converge fast; if they're three we have to choose, and that choice has weeks of consequences.
Move on.

Next week's commitment: by Wednesday we will have either (a) a single user-need statement that absorbs all three clusters or (b) an explicit segmentation rationale that picks one cluster as primary. Observable signal: the statement or rationale is written in our Notion doc and the four of us have signed off on it in writing.
I think we're done. Anything else you need?

On the bot — yeah, this surfaced way more honest reflection than the PDF, especially the team part. The PDF I'd have skimmed; here I had to actually answer with examples. Improvement: maybe show me a small 'section 4 of 6' indicator so I know how much is left.
